food_subgroup: Spiced black tea brewed with milk and sugar

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no specific concept for Masala Chai. Nearest parent is a spiced milk tea beverage. A new concept for South Asian masala chai preparations is recommended.
ontology.foodex2_description: Spiced black tea with milk (masala chai) — closest parent match
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No exact FoodEx2 code exists for masala chai; A15NT (spiced/flavoured tea with milk) used as closest defensible parent.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for masala chai as of April 2026.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard cup of masala chai (approximately 210 g) as typically served at Indian restaurants, mamak stalls, and teh tarik counters.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods — ICMR-NIN, derived from standard masala chai recipe component data
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research — National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values derived by recipe-component calculation using ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 edition) for black tea, full-fat cow's milk, sugar, ginger, cardamom, cinnamon, cloves, and black pepper in standard masala chai proportions. Values cross-checked against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for individual ingredients.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Sweetened Milk Tea — analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database (sweetened milk tea entries)
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 42 estimated from University of Sydney GI Database entries for sweetened milk tea. Milk protein (casein and whey) moderates glycaemic response relative to plain sugar solution. Masala chai shares the same base matrix (black tea + full-fat milk + sugar) as the analogous reference food. Glycaemic load per 210 g serving = (42 × 9.8 × 2.10) / 100 ≈ 8.6, rounded to 8.7 per input record. Confidence 0.9.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium content (~32 mg/100 g; ~67 mg per 210 g cup) with beneficial potassium (88 mg/100 g) and calcium (65 mg/100 g) from milk.
hypertension.guidance: Masala chai is suitable for people with hypertension; its sodium content is negligible and the potassium and calcium from full-fat milk actively support healthy blood pressure regulation.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low GI (~42) but moderate sugar content (8.8 g/100 g; ~18.5 g per 210 g cup); glycaemic load per cup is approximately 8.7.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes may consume masala chai in moderation — the low GI and milk-protein matrix limit glycaemic excursion, but the added sugar (18.5 g per cup) should be counted toward daily carbohydrate targets; reducing or substituting sugar is recommended for tighter glycaemic control.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (1.0 g/100 g), negligible trans fat (0.0 g/100 g), and anti-inflammatory spice compounds (gingerols, cinnamaldehyde, eugenol, piperine) with no cholesterol-raising concern at typical serving size.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Masala chai is broadly suitable for people with cardiovascular disease; the anti-inflammatory spice blend (ginger, cardamom, cinnamon, cloves, black pepper) may confer cardioprotective benefits, and the low saturated fat and sodium profile do not adversely affect lipid or blood pressure targets at one-cup servings.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Combination of ginger, cardamom, cinnamon, cloves, and black pepper — all with documented anti-inflammatory properties.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Masala chai is suitable for people with rheumatoid arthritis; the spice blend provides one of the most potent anti-inflammatory combinations in culinary use, with gingerols, shogaols, cinnamaldehyde, eugenol, and piperine all demonstrating NF-κB pathway modulation in peer-reviewed literature.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Masala Chai is India's iconic warming spiced milk tea, widely regarded as the country's national beverage. It is prepared by simmering black tea leaves (typically CTC Assam or Darjeeling) directly with whole aromatic spices — fresh ginger, green cardamom pods, cinnamon stick, cloves, and black pepper — in water, then adding full-fat milk and sugar and simmering the combined mixture for a further 2–3 minutes before straining into cups. The result is a rich, amber-coloured, milky beverage with an intensely fragrant, warming, and mildly spicy character. The spice blend varies by region and household: ginger and cardamom are universal, while cinnamon, cloves, and pepper are common additions. In Singapore, masala chai is widely available at Indian restaurants, mamak stalls, and teh tarik counters. Nutritionally, a 210 g cup provides approximately 130 kcal, 3.8 g protein, 3.4 g fat, and 20.6 g carbohydrate (18.5 g sugar). The GI is approximately 42 (low), with a glycaemic load of ~8.7 per cup. The spice compounds — gingerols, cinnamaldehyde, eugenol, and piperine — have documented anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties. Masala chai is vegetarian, halal, and gluten-free. It is suitable for most adults including those with hypertension and cardiovascular disease; people with Type 2 Diabetes should moderate sugar intake or request reduced-sugar preparation.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values derived from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods using standard masala chai recipe component data, cross-checked against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for individual ingredients. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database analogous sweetened milk tea entries with documented basis. All core macros, secondary nutrients, and micronutrients are populated from approved sources.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for masala chai as a prepared beverage is not available in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central as of April 2026. water_g set to -1: moisture content for the final brewed beverage is not directly reported in ICMR-NIN component tables. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero — full-fat cow's milk and plant spices contain negligible industrial trans fats; confirmed per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy milk entries). dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero — the beverage is strained before serving; insoluble fibre from spices is removed; confirmed per ICMR-NIN). vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed zero — full-fat cow's milk in India is not routinely fortified with vitamin D; confirmed per ICMR-NIN). GI value of 42 is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database sweetened milk tea entries; direct masala chai GI measurement not available in the database as of April 2026. FoodEx2 code A15NT used as closest defensible parent; no exact masala chai code exists in FoodEx2 as of April 2026. FoodOn URI is null — no specific FoodOn concept for masala chai exists; gap note provided. SNOMED CT code is null — no SNOMED CT concept found for masala chai as of April 2026.
